name: williams-r
whatItMeasures: Williams %R is a momentum oscillator that measures the current closing price relative to the high-low range over a lookback period. It oscillates between 0 and -100. Developed by Larry Williams, it is essentially an inverted Stochastic Oscillator and is particularly effective at identifying overbought and oversold conditions with precise timing.

parameters.name: Period
parameters.rationale: The 14-period lookback is the standard setting, balancing sensitivity to recent price action with enough data to avoid excessive noise. This was Larry Williams' original recommendation.

howToRead: Williams %R below -80 indicates the asset is oversold — the current price is near the bottom of its recent range.
Williams %R above -20 indicates the asset is overbought — the current price is near the top of its recent range.
A move from below -80 back above -80 is a potential buy signal (oversold recovery).
A move from above -20 back below -20 is a potential sell signal (overbought reversal).
The indicator ranges from 0 (highest high) to -100 (lowest low); readings around -50 are neutral.

signalRules.buy: Williams %R crosses below -80 (oversold zone), especially when it then turns back above -80.
signalRules.sell: Williams %R crosses above -20 (overbought zone), especially when it then turns back below -20.
signalRules.neutral: Williams %R between -80 and -20 with no extreme reading or directional shift.

limitations: Like RSI, Williams %R can remain in overbought or oversold territory for extended periods during strong trends.
The indicator is very sensitive to the lookback period — small changes can significantly alter readings.
Williams %R does not account for volume or broader market context.
In strongly trending markets, counter-trend signals from Williams %R will generate frequent false signals.
Best used as a confirmation tool alongside trend indicators rather than as a standalone system.
evidence: Backtesting on the S&P 500 by Liberatedstocktrader.com showed Williams %R achieving an 81% win rate with an 11.9% compound annual growth rate (CAGR). This makes it one of the highest-performing single oscillator indicators tested. Its effectiveness stems from its ability to precisely identify exhaustion points in short-term price swings within the context of broader trends.
